title: scikit-learn integration
text:
RieszBooster, KernelRieszRegressor, ForestRieszRegressor, and AugForestRieszRegressor are all subclasses of rieszreg.RieszEstimator, which inherits from sklearn.base.BaseEstimator. They compose with cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline, clone, and the rest of sklearn.model_selection.
This page uses RieszBooster; the same patterns work for the kernel and forest convenience classes with backend-specific hyperparameters.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Cross-fitting
Plug-in \(\hat\alpha\) in a one-step / TMLE / DML estimator must be out-of-fold. Use cross_val_predict:

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_predict
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 2000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=2000,
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=4,
)

alpha_oof = cross_val_predict(booster, df, cv=5)
alpha_oof.shape, alpha_oof[:5]

((2000,), array([-3.6196816, -1.6876634, -1.0861515, -1.0516312,  1.2505229],
      dtype=float32))





set.seed(0)
n  <- 2000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(-0.02 * x - x^2 + 4 * log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

# Call sklearn directly via reticulate, passing the underlying Python
# estimator from `booster$py`.
sk_ms <- reticulate::import("sklearn.model_selection", convert = FALSE)
booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 2000L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 4L
)
alpha_oof <- as.numeric(reticulate::py_to_r(
  sk_ms$cross_val_predict(booster$py, reticulate::r_to_py(df), cv = 5L)
))
length(alpha_oof); head(alpha_oof)

[1] 2000


[1]  1.301584  6.205725  2.930262 -3.024208  1.465699 -1.293439





Each fold runs its own internal early-stopping split.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Hyperparameter search
.score(X) returns negative held-out Riesz loss, so GridSearchCV selects the configuration with the lowest loss.

PythonR



from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

grid = GridSearchCV(
    RieszBooster(
        estimand=ATE(),
        n_estimators=300,
        early_stopping_rounds=20,
        validation_fraction=0.2,
    ),
    param_grid={"learning_rate": [0.03, 0.05, 0.1], "max_depth": [3, 4, 5]},
    cv=3,
    n_jobs=1,
).fit(df)

print("best params:", grid.best_params_)

best params: {'learning_rate': 0.05, 'max_depth': 3}

print(f"best score (neg Riesz loss): {grid.best_score_:.4f}")

best score (neg Riesz loss): 5.8153

best = grid.best_estimator_
alpha_hat = best.predict(df)
alpha_hat[:5]

array([-2.770411 , -1.6573501, -1.064923 , -1.0326685,  1.2886328],
      dtype=float32)





sk_ms <- reticulate::import("sklearn.model_selection", convert = FALSE)
base  <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
grid <- sk_ms$GridSearchCV(
  base$py,
  param_grid = reticulate::dict(
    learning_rate = c(0.03, 0.05, 0.1),
    max_depth = list(3L, 4L, 5L)
  ),
  cv = 3L,
  n_jobs = 1L
)
grid$fit(reticulate::r_to_py(df))

GridSearchCV(cv=3,
             estimator=RieszBooster(early_stopping_rounds=20,
                                    estimand=Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'),
                                                      m=<function ATE.<locals>.m at 0x13e49e7a0>,
                                                      extra_keys=(),
                                                      name='ATE',
                                                      factory_spec={'args': {'covariates': ['x'],
                                                                             'treatment': 'a'},
                                                                    'factory': 'ATE'}),
                                    n_estimators=300, validation_fraction=0.2),
             n_jobs=1,
             param_grid={'learning_rate': [0.03, 0.05, 0.1],
                         'max_depth': [3, 4, 5]})

cat("best params:\n"); print(reticulate::py_to_r(grid$best_params_))

best params:


$learning_rate
[1] 0.1

$max_depth
[1] 3

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Other sklearn integrations
Every Riesz estimator exposes the standard BaseEstimator interface:

clone(estimator) returns a fresh unfitted copy with the same hyperparameters.
Pipeline wraps a feature-engineering step in front of the estimator.
HalvingGridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, BayesSearchCV (skopt) work without modification.
get_params() / set_params() are used by the search classes to introspect and mutate the configuration.

title: scikit-learn integration

section: Predict and compare to the closed form
text:
Predict and compare to the closed form
We know \(\alpha_0\) here, so we can score the fit directly.

PythonR



alpha_hat  = booster.predict(df)
true_alpha = a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
print(f"corr(α̂, α₀) = {np.corrcoef(alpha_hat, true_alpha)[0, 1]:.3f}")

corr(α̂, α₀) = 0.934

print(f"RMSE         = {np.sqrt(np.mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)**2)):.3f}")

RMSE         = 0.973





alpha_hat  <- booster$predict(df)
true_alpha <- a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
cat(sprintf("corr(alpha_hat, alpha_0) = %.3f\n",
            cor(alpha_hat, true_alpha)))

corr(alpha_hat, alpha_0) = 0.962

cat(sprintf("RMSE                     = %.3f\n",
            sqrt(mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)^2))))

RMSE                     = 0.768

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
text:
In semiparametric inference, you often want to estimate a functional of an unknown nuisance:
\[
\theta(P) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[m(Z, g_0)\right]
\]
where \(g_0(z) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z = z]\) is an outcome regression and \(m(z, g)\) is a linear functional of \(g\). Examples:







Target
\(m(z, g)\)




Average treatment effect
\(g(1, x) - g(0, x)\)


Treatment-specific mean at \(a^\star\)
\(g(a^\star, x)\)


Additive shift effect \(a \mapsto a + \delta\)
\(g(a + \delta, x) - g(a, x)\)


Stochastic intervention \(a \sim h(\cdot \mid x)\)
\(\int g(a', x)\,h(a' \mid x)\,da'\)



The plug-in estimator \(\hat\theta = n^{-1} \sum_i m(Z_i, \hat g)\) is biased because \(\hat g\) is itself estimated. Modern semiparametric estimators (one-step, TMLE, DML) correct that bias by adding a term involving the Riesz representer \(\alpha_0\) of \(m\):
\[
\hat\theta_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; n^{-1} \sum_i \Big[\,m(Z_i, \hat g) + \alpha_0(Z_i)\,\big(Y_i - \hat g(Z_i)\big)\,\Big].
\]
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
text:
What is \(\alpha_0\)?
By the Riesz representation theorem, since \(m(z, \cdot)\) is bounded and linear in \(g\), there exists a function \(\alpha_0(z)\) satisfying
\[
\mathbb{E}[m(Z, g)] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha_0(Z) \cdot g(Z)\right]
\quad \text{for all } g.
\]
For ATE, \(\alpha_0(a, x) = a/\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\pi(x))\) is the inverse-propensity weight. For TSM, \(\alpha_0\) is the density ratio \(\mathbb{1}\{a = a^\star\}/\pi(a^\star \mid x)\). In general, \(\alpha_0\) has a closed form involving the generalized propensity score.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
section: Why not estimate the propensity directly?
text:
Why not estimate the propensity directly?
For ATE you can: estimate \(\hat\pi(x)\), plug in, get \(\hat\alpha(a, x) = a/\hat\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\hat\pi(x))\).
This breaks down when:

Positivity is borderline. If \(\hat\pi(x)\) approaches 0 or 1, \(\hat\alpha\) explodes. Truncation patches the symptom but introduces its own bias.
The estimand is non-standard. For continuous shifts, stochastic interventions, and longitudinal LMTPs, the closed form involves derivatives of the conditional density of \(A\) given \(X\). Those are harder to estimate than \(\alpha_0\) itself.
Misspecification compounds. Plug-in error in \(\hat\pi\) propagates nonlinearly through \(1/\hat\pi\).
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
text:
Riesz regression
Chernozhukov et al. (2021) observed that \(\alpha_0\) is the unique minimizer of the Riesz loss
\[
L(\alpha) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha(Z)^2 - 2\,m(Z, \alpha)\right]
\]
over all square-integrable \(\alpha\). The Gateaux derivative at \(\alpha_0\) vanishes by the Riesz identity. Estimating \(\alpha_0\) becomes a regression problem with a custom loss: no propensity, no density derivative, no truncation.
The minimizer is well-defined regardless of the closed-form tails. Where \(\pi(x)\) is small, the squared-loss penalty shrinks \(\hat\alpha\) toward zero in low-overlap regions, which controls downstream variance.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
text:
Pick a fitter
Riesz regression with the squared loss is a standard regression problem. Different fitters trade off the same way they do for outcome regression:

Gradient boosting — the standard tool for tabular data. rieszboost plugs the per-row gradient and Hessian into xgboost’s custom-objective interface. Hyperparameters that work for outcome models transfer.
Kernel ridge regression — closed form on small/medium data, exactly satisfies the variational characterization. krrr solves the linear system per λ; no boosting iterations.
Random forests — non-parametric and well-suited to many covariates. forestriesz ships two flavors built on EconML’s GRF: a moment-style fit with honest-split confidence intervals, and an augmentation-style fit that handles every estimand (including custom ones) without a sieve.

The three backends produce different \(\hat\alpha\), but all are minimizers of the same population objective and converge to \(\alpha_0\) under their respective consistency conditions.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
text:
What rieszreg provides
A scikit-learn estimator (and an R6 mirror) that:

Fits \(\hat\alpha\) for ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention, or your own estimand.
Composes with cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline.
Surfaces near-positivity and outlier-extrapolation warnings via .diagnose(X).
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why fit one?
text:
Related work
A few existing tools cover overlapping ground.
genriesz (Kato, 2026) is a single Python package implementing the Bregman-unified Riesz regression framework from arXiv:2601.07752. It exposes LinearFunctional and BregmanGenerator abstractions, analogous to this project’s Estimand and LossSpec. It ships several basis-function classes (polynomial, random Fourier features, Nyström, KNN catchments, random-forest leaves, PyTorch embeddings) inside the package itself. Third parties cannot publish their own learners against a stable protocol. It is Python only.
EconML (Microsoft) provides RieszNet, ForestRiesz, and an automatic_debiased_ml module. The forestriesz package in this family wraps EconML’s BaseGRF. EconML is monolithic, with no third-party backend protocol, and Python only.
DoubleML (Bach, Chernozhukov, Kurz, Spindler) is a mature DML library with parallel Python and R implementations. It expects the user to supply outcome and propensity nuisances using sklearn-compatible learners. Riesz regression is not the focal abstraction.
tlverse (van der Laan group) is an R-only family of packages (sl3, tmle3, lmtp, hal9001, …) organized around TMLE and SuperLearner. The meta-package + sibling-backends shape is the closest organizational match to this project.
What’s distinctive here:

The Backend / MomentBackend split, exposed as a stable Protocol, lets a third-party learner package depend on rieszreg and ship as its own PyPI/CRAN release. New learners do not require a PR upstream.
The split itself reflects two structurally different fitting strategies: augmentation-style (kernel ridge, gradient boosting via fit_augmented) vs. moment-style (forests, neural nets via fit_rows).
Cross-language Python + R coverage at the family level via R6 wrappers per package, not just bindings to a Python core.

Next: install and the quickstart.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why fit one?

title: RieszReg
text:
The rieszreg family estimates the Riesz representer \(\alpha_0\) of a linear functional \(\theta(P) = \mathbb{E}[m(Z, g_0)]\), the building block of one-step, TMLE, and DML estimators in semiparametric inference.
The meta-package rieszreg holds the shared abstractions: Estimand factories, Bregman-Riesz LossSpecs, the augmentation engine, the Backend Protocol, and the sklearn-compatible RieszEstimator orchestrator. Implementation packages provide concrete backends:
R and Python predictions are bitwise-identical on the same input. Most pages present every code example in both languages; pick a tab and your choice persists across the site.

title: RieszReg
text:
Two ways to compose
# Convenience class with sensible defaults baked in:
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import ATE
booster = RieszBooster(estimand=ATE(), n_estimators=200)
booster.fit(X)

# Or compose explicitly: pick any backend, pass it to RieszEstimator
from rieszreg import RieszEstimator, ATE, SquaredLoss
from rieszboost.backends import XGBoostBackend
est = RieszEstimator(estimand=ATE(), loss=SquaredLoss(), backend=XGBoostBackend())
est.fit(X)
Both routes are first-class. The convenience class is just RieszEstimator with the backend defaulted.

TipCode execution



Every code block in this guide ran at build time. The numbers and figures came from the version of rieszreg, rieszboost, krrr, and forestriesz that produced this page.

title: Diagnostics
text:
You don’t observe \(\alpha_0\) on real data, so the validation curve from early_stopping_rounds is the only training-time signal. After fitting, .diagnose() reports magnitude statistics and warnings about extreme tail values, near-positivity violations, and single-point extrapolation. Those warnings often matter more for downstream variance than the bulk fit quality.
.diagnose() is defined in rieszreg.diagnostics and works for any RieszEstimator subclass. Backend-specific extensions (e.g. KernelDiagnostics adds chosen λ, support size, effective d.o.f., and the kernel matrix’s condition number) layer on top of the shared base.
This page walks through .diagnose() on an under-tuned booster (so the warnings fire) and maps each warning to the hyperparameter that fixes it. See Boosting backend and Kernel backend for the backend-specific tuning recipes.

title: Diagnostics
text:
Setup: a low-overlap dataset
We push the propensity close to 0 in part of the covariate space. This is where naive inverse-propensity weighting falls apart.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 2000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8.0 * x - 5.0)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})
print(f"min π(x) = {pi.min():.3f}, fraction with π(x) < 0.05 = {(pi < 0.05).mean():.2f}")

min π(x) = 0.007, fraction with π(x) < 0.05 = 0.26





set.seed(0)
n  <- 2000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)
cat(sprintf("min pi(x) = %.3f, fraction with pi(x) < 0.05 = %.2f\n",
            min(pi), mean(pi < 0.05)))

min pi(x) = 0.007, fraction with pi(x) < 0.05 = 0.26

title: Diagnostics
text:
Reading the report
The summary has three parts.
Magnitude block. RMS, mean, min/max, and |alpha| quantiles. The 99th percentile and the max are the load-bearing numbers. Healthy fits have max no more than a few times the 99th percentile. Max far above the 99th percentile signals a single-point extrapolation outlier: one leaf is far from any training data and its prediction has run away.
Extreme rows. Rows where \(|\hat\alpha|\) exceeds extreme_threshold (default 30). Downstream IF estimator variance is dominated by the largest few weights, so even 1% of rows over the threshold inflates confidence intervals.
Warnings. Two automatic checks:

“X% of rows have \(|\hat\alpha| >\) threshold — possible near-positivity violation.” Either the data has genuine near-positivity (truncate extreme_threshold and report the loss of overlap), or the estimator is over-fitting (tune as below).
“max \(|\hat\alpha|\) is >10× the 99th percentile — likely a single extrapolation outlier.” The fitter found a region with no nearby training data. Tighten model complexity, increase regularization, or stop earlier.

title: Diagnostics
text:
What .diagnose() does not do
It does not estimate \(\alpha_0\) for comparison; that closed form is generally unavailable. It does not assess sample size. It checks magnitude and the two failure modes above. For fit quality, track the held-out Riesz loss (best_score_). For downstream confidence intervals, use the bootstrap on the plug-in estimator.

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
krrr provides KernelRidgeBackend and the KernelRieszRegressor convenience class. It implements Singh, Kernel Ridge Riesz Representers (arXiv:2102.11076) for the full set of estimands, by piping rieszreg’s augmentation engine into a closed-form kernel solve.
The augmented dataset gives per-row coefficients \(a_k\) and \(b_k\). The squared Riesz loss with kernel ridge regularization
\[
L_n(\alpha) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_k \big[ a_k\,\alpha(p_k)^2 + b_k\,\alpha(p_k) \big] + \lambda\,\|\alpha\|^2_\mathcal{H}
\]
has the representer-theorem solution \(\hat\alpha = \sum_k \gamma_k\,k(\cdot, p_k)\) with \(\gamma\) solving the linear system
\[
(\mathrm{diag}(a)\,K + n\lambda\,I)\,\gamma \;=\; -b/2.
\]
Partitioning into \(o = \{a_k > 0\}\) (original rows) and \(c = \{a_k = 0\}\) (counterfactual evaluation points): \(\gamma_c\) is closed form, and \(\gamma_o\) solves a symmetric PSD system on the o-block. A single eigendecomposition of the rescaled kernel matrix solves the entire λ path.

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
Quickstart

PythonR

    validation_fraction=0.25,

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
Kernels
krrr ships eight kernels with algebra (Sum, Product, Scaled, Tensor):
from krrr import Gaussian, Matern, Linear, Polynomial, Tensor

Gaussian(length_scale="median")          # default; median pairwise distance
Gaussian(length_scale="scott")            # Scott's rule
Gaussian(length_scale=0.5)                # fixed
Matern(nu=2.5, length_scale="median")     # ν ∈ {0.5, 1.5, 2.5}
Linear()                                  # k(x, y) = x · y
Polynomial(degree=3, gamma=1.0, coef0=1.0)

# Algebra
Gaussian() + Linear()                     # Sum
0.5 * Gaussian()                           # Scaled
Gaussian() * Linear()                      # Product (Hadamard on Gram)
Tensor(Gaussian(), [0, 1], Linear(), [2])  # tensor product over disjoint columns
Length-scale "median", "scott", and "silverman" heuristics resolve at fit time on the augmented training points, so the bandwidth adapts to whatever scale matters for this dataset.

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
Solver tier








Solver
When to use
Cost




"direct"
n_aug ≤ 3,000
One eigendecomposition; entire λ-path is O(n²) per λ. Exact.


"nystrom_cg"
n_aug ≤ 50,000
Preconditioned CG on the o-block; m landmarks.


"rff"
n_aug very large; shift-invariant kernel
Primal D × D solve via random Fourier features.


"falkon"
n_aug very large; GPU available
Wraps the optional falkon package.


"auto"
default
Dispatches by n_aug.



The solver consumes the augmented dataset directly; you never deal with kernel matrices yourself.






WarningFalkon limitation



The Falkon backend currently drops the \(K_{oc}\,b_c\) coupling on the o-block — Falkon’s standalone API only solves vanilla KRR, not the modified-RHS system the augmentation produces. For estimands where \(n_c\) is small or λ is moderate the bias is small; for tight overlap or extreme λ it is not. Use solver="nystrom_cg" if exactness matters more than scale.

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
λ selection
Pass a grid of λ values; the backend selects by validation Riesz loss when validation_fraction > 0 or eval_set is given.
krr = KernelRieszRegressor(
    estimand=ATE(),
    lambda_grid=np.logspace(-5, 1, 31),
    validation_fraction=0.25,
).fit(df)
print(krr.lambda_)   # the chosen λ
For consistency theory, λ should scale \(O(1/n)\). Cross-fitting users should re-tune per fold (sklearn’s cross_val_predict does this if KernelRieszRegressor is wrapped in GridSearchCV).

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
Loss support
KernelRidgeBackend currently supports SquaredLoss only — non-quadratic losses (KLLoss, BernoulliLoss, BoundedSquaredLoss) require Newton iteration on the kernel system, planned for v0.2. Pass an unsupported loss and the backend raises at fit time with a clear error.
For non-quadratic losses today, use the boosting backend.

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
Reference parity
krrr exercises a TSM1 numerical-parity test against the dml-tmle R reference at tolerance 1e-8.

title: Kernel backend (krrr)
text:
Sharp edges

Median-heuristic bandwidth on the augmented dataset. The median is computed on augmented points (originals + counterfactuals from \(m\)). For shift-style estimands this includes the shifted treatment values.
solver="falkon" drops \(K_{oc}\,b_c\). See callout above.

title: Forest backend (forestriesz)
text:
forestriesz ships two backends, both built on EconML’s BaseGRF (the same Cython criterion the paper authors used):
rieszreg exposes the two entry points as MomentBackend.fit_rows and Backend.fit_augmented respectively.

title: Forest backend (forestriesz)
text:
How they work

Moment-style (ForestRieszRegressor)
In each leaf \(\ell\) the per-leaf parameter \(\theta_\ell\) solves the linear-moment equation
\[
\hat\theta_\ell = \big(\textstyle\sum_{i\in\ell} \varphi(W_i)\,\varphi(W_i)^{\top}\big)^{-1}
                 \textstyle\sum_{i\in\ell} m(W_i;\,\varphi),
\]
and predictions are \(\hat\alpha(z) = \hat\theta(z_{\text{split}}) \cdot \varphi(z)\). The MSE splitting criterion picks splits to minimize the sum of in-leaf residuals against this leaf optimum.
The user-supplied basis \(\varphi = (\varphi_1, \dots, \varphi_p)\) controls expressivity. The default riesz_feature_fns="auto" picks a sensible sieve for built-in estimands:








Estimand
Default sieve
Effect




ATE, ATT
[1{T=0}, 1{T=1}]
Forest splits on covariates only; sieve resolves treatment in each leaf.


TSM(level=v)
[1{T=v}]
Single-basis sieve; intervals supported.


AdditiveShift, LocalShift, custom
none
Constant basis is row-degenerate; raises with a hint. Use AugForestRieszRegressor instead.





Augmentation-style (AugForestRieszRegressor)
The augmentation engine produces M = k·n evaluation points \(z_k\) with coefficients \((a_k, b_k)\) such that the squared Riesz loss is \(\sum_k a_k\,\alpha(z_k)^2 + b_k\,\alpha(z_k)\). Per augmented row:
\[
J_k = 2 a_k \cdot \varphi(z_k)\varphi(z_k)^{\top}, \qquad A_k = -b_k \cdot \varphi(z_k).
\]
Originals contribute \(a_k = 1, b_k = 0\) so \(J_k\) varies across rows; counterfactual evaluation points contribute \(a_k = 0, b_k \neq 0\) so \(A_k\) varies. The forest can split the full feature space usefully even with the constant basis \(\varphi \equiv 1\). For ATE the leaf solve recovers \(1/\hat P(T = t \mid X\text{-leaf})\); for AdditiveShift it recovers a local density-ratio estimator.
The trade-off is that the augmented training rows aren’t iid — there are blocks of \(k\) correlated rows per original \(W_i\) — so GRF’s variance estimator no longer applies and predict_interval is not exposed in v1. Cluster-robust variance with origin_index as the cluster id is on the v2 roadmap.

title: Forest backend (forestriesz)
text:
How they differ









ForestRieszRegressor
AugForestRieszRegressor




Estimand support
ATE/ATT/TSM via auto sieve; others need user-supplied sieve
Every built-in + custom user Estimand, no sieve


predict_interval
yes (single-basis fits, honest=True)
not in v1


Per-fit speed
baseline
≈1.0× at n≤2000, ≈1.15× at n=5000


RMSE on shared estimands (ATE, TSM)
baseline
within ~5%


Splits on
covariates only when sieve handles treatment
full (T, X) feature space


Backend Protocol
MomentBackend.fit_rows
Backend.fit_augmented



Default to ForestRieszRegressor for ATE/ATT/TSM when you want CIs. Reach for AugForestRieszRegressor for shift-style estimands, custom moments, or when you want one estimator that just works on everything. See test_aug_vs_moment.py for the benchmark behind the speed and RMSE numbers.

section: Quickstart — AdditiveShift with the augmentation-style backend

The same estimator works on every built-in estimand at near-identical speed and quality vs the moment-style backend; trade off CIs for full agnosticism.

Save / load

import tempfile, os

title: Forest backend (forestriesz)
text:
Sklearn integration
ForestRieszRegressor is a BaseEstimator, so it composes with clone, GridSearchCV, cross_val_predict, Pipeline. See sklearn integration.

title: Forest backend (forestriesz)
text:
When the constant basis raises
For built-in estimands the per-row moment \(m(W; 1)\) doesn’t depend on \(W\), so the constant basis is degenerate (every leaf would predict the same constant). The backend detects this and raises with a hint to use riesz_feature_fns="auto" (the default) or supply a custom sieve. The default does the right thing; explicit riesz_feature_fns=None is what raises.

title: Boosting backend (rieszboost)
text:
rieszboost provides two boosting backends and the RieszBooster convenience class. XGBoostBackend is the default; SklearnBackend runs Friedman gradient boosting against any sklearn-compatible base learner.
The augmentation engine produces per-row quadratic (\(a\)) and linear (\(b\)) coefficients. Each original row contributes \(a=1\), \(b=0\); each \((c, p)\) pair from \(m(z, \cdot)\) contributes \(a=0\), \(b=-2c\). The Riesz loss becomes a per-row weighted regression that xgboost handles via its custom-objective interface.

title: Boosting backend (rieszboost)
text:
XGBoostBackend
The default. Boosts in η-space; the predictor applies the loss spec’s link to convert to α.
XGBoostBackend(hessian_floor=2.0, gradient_only=False)
hessian_floor is the lower bound on the per-row Hessian fed to xgboost. Counterfactual rows have \(H = 2a = 0\); without a floor, xgboost’s leaf-weight Newton step (-G/(H+λ)) is degenerate at those leaves. The default of 2.0 matches the natural Hessian on original rows.
gradient_only short-circuits the second-order Hessian and uses \(H = 1\) everywhere — first-order gradient boosting (Friedman 2001 / Lee-Schuler Algorithm 2). Set True to reproduce the Lee-Schuler reference implementation exactly.
max_depth, reg_lambda, subsample, learning_rate, n_estimators, and early_stopping_rounds are passed through from the RieszBooster constructor.

Knob
Default

Effect




max_depth

Shallow trees can’t carve out a high-magnitude leaf for a single point.


learning_rate

Smaller updates per tree; pair with higher n_estimators.

The validation split picks the iteration count.

title: Boosting backend (rieszboost)
text:
SklearnBackend
To use a non-tree base learner, pass a base_learner_factory: a zero-arg callable returning a fresh sklearn-compatible regressor each round.
The backend implements first-order gradient boosting with closed-form line search. Each round: fit the weak learner to the negative gradient of the Riesz loss, solve for the optimal step size under a quadratic surrogate, update the running prediction.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from sklearn.kernel_ridge import KernelRidge
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, SklearnBackend
from rieszreg import ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    backend=SklearnBackend(lambda: KernelRidge(alpha=1.0, kernel="rbf", gamma=2.0)),
    n_estimators=80,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    early_stopping_rounds=10,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat  = booster.predict(df)
true_alpha = a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
print(f"corr = {np.corrcoef(alpha_hat, true_alpha)[0,1]:.3f}, "
      f"RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)**2)):.3f}")

corr = 0.913, RMSE = 1.663





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

# Build the Python factory directly via reticulate so each round gets a
# fresh sklearn object.
sk_kr <- reticulate::import("sklearn.kernel_ridge", convert = FALSE)
factory <- reticulate::py_func(function() sk_kr$KernelRidge(alpha = 1.0,
                                                             kernel = "rbf",
                                                             gamma = 2.0))

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  backend = SklearnBackend(base_learner_factory = factory),
  n_estimators = 80L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  early_stopping_rounds = 10L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
booster$fit(df)

alpha_hat  <- booster$predict(df)
true_alpha <- a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
cat(sprintf("corr = %.3f, RMSE = %.3f\n",
            cor(alpha_hat, true_alpha),
            sqrt(mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)^2))))

corr = 0.928, RMSE = 1.579





SklearnBackend is slower than XGBoostBackend (no parallel tree splits). Use it when the data is better fit by a non-tree learner (e.g. low-dimensional problems where kernel ridge dominates), or when xgboost is unavailable in your environment.






TipSkipping xgboost



SklearnBackend does not import xgboost. The library lazy-imports xgboost only when XGBoostBackend is used, so you can run rieszboost in xgboost-hostile environments (alpine containers, builds without OpenMP) by passing backend=SklearnBackend(...) and never installing xgboost.

title: Boosting backend (rieszboost)
text:
Reference parity
rieszboost cross-checks against Lee’s reference implementation. With gradient_only=True, learning_rate = lr_ref / 2, and reg_lambda=0, predictions match to Pearson 0.998 / 0.986 on the binary-DGP example. See rieszboost/examples/lee_schuler/COMPARISON.md for the full report.

title: Backends
text: A backend fits the Riesz representer for a given estimand and loss. Three implementation packages ship today; each slots into RieszEstimator through one of two Protocols. rieszboost and krrr consume the augmented (a, b) dataset (Backend.fit_augmented). forestriesz ships both: a moment-style ForestRieszRegressor that consumes original rows + per-row moments (MomentBackend.fit_rows), and an augmentation-style AugForestRieszRegressor that consumes the augmented dataset directly (Backend.fit_augmented).

text:
Pick a backend

Convenience class
rieszboost.RieszBooster
krrr.KernelRieszRegressor
forestriesz.ForestRieszRegressor (moment-style) and forestriesz.AugForestRieszRegressor (augmentation-style, fully estimand-agnostic)

title: Backends
text:
Compose explicitly
Both convenience classes are subclasses of rieszreg.RieszEstimator with the backend defaulted. To swap backends or build your own, compose explicitly:
from rieszreg import RieszEstimator, ATE, SquaredLoss
from rieszboost.backends import XGBoostBackend, SklearnBackend
from krrr import KernelRidgeBackend, Gaussian
from forestriesz import ForestRieszBackend

# Default: xgboost
est = RieszEstimator(estimand=ATE(), backend=XGBoostBackend())

# Sklearn boosting with KernelRidge as the base learner
from sklearn.kernel_ridge import KernelRidge
est = RieszEstimator(
    estimand=ATE(),
    backend=SklearnBackend(lambda: KernelRidge(alpha=1.0, kernel="rbf", gamma=2.0)),
)

# Kernel ridge directly
est = RieszEstimator(
    estimand=ATE(),
    backend=KernelRidgeBackend(kernel=Gaussian(length_scale="median")),
)

# Random forest (moment-style backend; fit_rows path)
est = RieszEstimator(
    estimand=ATE(),
    backend=ForestRieszBackend(n_estimators=500),
)
The convenience classes are thin subclasses that bake in the backend and surface backend-specific hyperparameters as constructor args, so that they fit cleanly into GridSearchCV. See the boosting, kernel, and forest pages for each.

title: Backends
text:
Adding a backend
Implement one of two Protocols from rieszreg.backends.base:

Backend.fit_augmented(aug_train, aug_valid, loss, …) — for augmentation-style learners (kernel ridge, gradient boosting). Implementations: KernelRidgeBackend, XGBoostBackend, SklearnBackend.
MomentBackend.fit_rows(rows_train, rows_valid, estimand, loss, …) — for moment-style learners (random forests, neural nets). Compute per-row moments via rieszreg.trace(estimand, row). Implementation: ForestRieszBackend.

Either way, return a FitResult whose predictor implements predict_eta and predict_alpha. Register the predictor’s loader for round-trip via:
from rieszreg.backends import register_predictor_loader
register_predictor_loader("my-kind", MyPredictor.load)
See Developing for the full contract.

title: Developing
text:
Tracer
The user writes m(z, alpha) with +, -, scalar *, and calls to alpha(...). rieszreg.estimands.tracer passes a Tracer in as alpha; each call returns a single-term LinearForm, and Python’s operator overloading composes them. The result has .as_pairs() returning [(coef, point), ...], which build_augmented consumes. Operations outside the linear-form algebra (squaring, integrals, constants) raise at trace time.

title: Developing
Developing
text:
Adding a LossSpec
Losses also belong in rieszreg, not the backends.

Add the class to rieszreg/python/rieszreg/losses/<name>.py.
Wire into rieszreg/python/rieszreg/losses/__init__.py (export + loss_from_spec registry).
Add R wrappers.
Update docs/losses.qmd with a runnable example.
Test in rieszreg/python/tests/.
Implementation packages whose backend supports the new loss should add a smoke test confirming it works on a small DGP.

title: Developing
text:
Adding a backend
Pick the entry point that matches your learner’s natural loss decomposition. Both Protocols live in rieszreg/python/rieszreg/backends/base.py and both return a FitResult whose predictor implements predict_eta and predict_alpha.
Augmentation-style — Backend.fit_augmented(aug_train, aug_valid, loss, …). For learners that minimize a per-evaluation-point loss (kernel ridge, gradient boosting). The orchestrator hands you a precomputed AugmentedDataset of (a, b) coefficients at evaluation points; you don’t see the estimand. Reference implementations: XGBoostBackend, SklearnBackend (rieszboost), KernelRidgeBackend (krrr), AugForestRieszBackend (forestriesz).
Moment-style — MomentBackend.fit_rows(rows_train, rows_valid, estimand, loss, …). For learners that minimize a per-original-row loss (random forests, neural nets). You receive raw rows + the estimand and call rieszreg.trace(estimand, row) per row to compute moments. Reference implementation: ForestRieszBackend (forestriesz).
Implementations advertise at most one method; the orchestrator dispatches via hasattr and defaults to fit_augmented if both are present.
Register the predictor’s loader so save/load round-trip works:
from rieszreg.backends import register_predictor_loader
register_predictor_loader("my-kind", MyPredictor.load)
A new implementation package (e.g. rieszneural) follows the contract in rieszreg/DESIGN.md, Part B: depend on rieszreg, implement one of the two Protocols, satisfy the sklearn-conformance subset, contribute docs pages here.

title: Developing
text:
Living-doc rule
Public-API changes update both README.md and the relevant docs/*.qmd page in the same commit. On bilingual pages, update both the Python and R tabs.
The pre-commit hook at .githooks/pre-commit (currently per-package; a meta-project version is on the roadmap) enforces this. Activate per clone:
git config core.hooksPath .githooks
The hook also greps the staged diff for two doc anti-patterns and blocks if it finds them: design-decision metacommentary (sentences describing the API’s negative space, e.g. what’s omitted, deferred, or rejected) and AI-flavored hedges (editorial framing, sweeping qualifiers, ornamental noun phrases). Match the ngboost user guide tone: direct, instructive, short.
For internal refactors that don’t touch any signature, test additions, or comment fixes: git commit --no-verify.

title: Estimands
text:
An Estimand describes the linear functional you want to fit. It holds three pieces of information: the column names \(\alpha\) is indexed by (feature_keys), any per-row payload columns \(m\) needs such as pre-sampled treatments for stochastic interventions (extra_keys), and the m(z, alpha) callable.
This page covers the six built-in factories and how to write your own. The factories live in rieszreg; rieszboost, krrr, and forestriesz all re-export them so from rieszboost import ATE, from krrr import ATE, and from forestriesz import ATE resolve to the same object.

title: Estimands
text:
Built-in factories



Factory
\(m(z, \alpha)\)
Notes




ATE(treatment, covariates)
\(\alpha(1, x) - \alpha(0, x)\)
Average treatment effect — see rieszboost/examples/lee_schuler/binary_dgp.py


ATT(treatment, covariates)
\(a \cdot (\alpha(1, x) - \alpha(0, x))\)
ATT partial parameter. Full ATT divides by \(P(A=1)\) — combine with a delta-method EIF downstream.


TSM(level, treatment, covariates)
\(\alpha(\text{level}, x)\)
Treatment-specific mean — see rieszboost/examples/tsm.py


AdditiveShift(delta, ...)
\(\alpha(a + \delta, x) - \alpha(a, x)\)
Continuous-treatment shift effect — see rieszboost/examples/lee_schuler/continuous_dgp.py


LocalShift(delta, threshold, ...)
\(\mathbf{1}\{a < \text{threshold}\} \cdot (\alpha(a + \delta, x) - \alpha(a, x))\)
LASE partial parameter; same caveat as ATT.


StochasticIntervention(samples_key, ...)
\(\frac{1}{K} \sum_k \alpha(a'_k, x)\)
IPSI via Monte Carlo over the intervention density — see rieszboost/examples/stochastic_intervention.py



The R and Python factories take the same arguments.

PythonR



from rieszreg import (
    ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention,
)
ATE(treatment="a", covariates=("x",)).name

'ATE'

TSM(level=1, treatment="a", covariates=("x",)).name

'TSM(level=1)'

LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7).name

'LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7)'





ATE(treatment = "a", covariates = "x")

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function ATE.<locals>.m at 0x116c61620>, extra_keys=(), name='ATE', factory_spec={'factory': 'ATE', 'args': {'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

TSM(level = 1, treatment = "a", covariates = "x")

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function TSM.<locals>.m at 0x13050ade0>, extra_keys=(), name='TSM(level=1.0)', factory_spec={'factory': 'TSM', 'args': {'level': 1.0, 'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

LocalShift(delta = 0.3, threshold = 0.7)

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function LocalShift.<locals>.m at 0x1305180e0>, extra_keys=(), name='LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7)', factory_spec={'factory': 'LocalShift', 'args': {'delta': 0.3, 'threshold': 0.7, 'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

title: Estimands
text:
Stochastic intervention: pre-sample, then fit
StochasticIntervention needs a list of treatment values drawn from the intervention density on each row. Build that column once before fitting.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import StochasticIntervention

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
a = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a, "x": x})

# Modified-treatment policy: a' ~ Normal(a + 0.1, 0.2), 20 samples per row
df["shift_samples"] = [
    rng.normal(a_i + 0.1, 0.2, size=20) for a_i in df["a"]
]

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=StochasticIntervention(samples_key="shift_samples"),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
).fit(df)

booster.predict(df).mean()

np.float32(0.35250518)





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n); a <- runif(n)
df <- data.frame(a = a, x = x)

# list-column with 20 samples per row from Normal(a + 0.1, 0.2)
df$shift_samples <- lapply(df$a, function(a_i) rnorm(20, a_i + 0.1, 0.2))

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = StochasticIntervention(samples_key = "shift_samples"),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 4L
)
booster$fit(df)
mean(booster$predict(df))

[1] 0.3499112





StochasticIntervention sets extra_keys=("shift_samples",) so the estimator pulls that column through to m along with the features.

title: Estimands
text:
Custom estimands
To fit a functional not covered by the built-ins, write m(z, alpha) and wrap it in an Estimand. The library traces \(m\) to extract the linear-form structure.
\(m\) must be a finite linear combination of point evaluations of \(\alpha\), built with +, -, scalar *, and calls to alpha(...). Squaring, adding constants, integrals, and other non-linear operations raise.






NotePython-only



Custom m() is Python-only. From R, define m in Python via reticulate::py_run_string and pass the resulting Estimand to your estimator. See the R interface page for the recipe.



from rieszreg import Estimand
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
import numpy as np, pandas as pd

# A weighted contrast: 2x the ATE at level a=2 minus the level at a=1.
def m_weighted_contrast(z, alpha):
    return 2.0 * (alpha(a=2, x=z["x"]) - alpha(a=0, x=z["x"])) \
           - alpha(a=1, x=z["x"])

est = Estimand(
    feature_keys=("a", "x"),
    m=m_weighted_contrast,
    name="WeightedContrast",
)
print(est)

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function m_weighted_contrast at 0x1311fce00>, extra_keys=(), name='WeightedContrast', factory_spec=None)


Pass the estimand to any backend’s estimator class:

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "a": rng.choice([0, 1, 2], size=500).astype(float),
    "x": rng.uniform(0, 1, 500),
})

booster = RieszBooster(estimand=est, n_estimators=100, max_depth=3).fit(df)
booster.predict(df)[:5]

array([ 4.630866 , -2.446456 , -2.5141447, -4.992177 , -4.992177 ],
      dtype=float32)



What the tracer rejects
The errors point at the offending operation:

from rieszreg import Estimand, trace

def bad_squared(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) ** 2  # nonlinear in alpha

trace(Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=bad_squared), {"a": 0, "x": 0.5})

TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for ** or pow(): 'LinearForm' and 'int'



def bad_constant(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) + 1.0  # constant offset

trace(Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=bad_constant), {"a": 0, "x": 0.5})

TypeError: Cannot add a scalar to a LinearForm — m must be linear in alpha (no constant offsets).


For estimands with integrals or derivatives of \(\alpha\) (outside the finite-linear-form algebra), Monte-Carlo-sample and use StochasticIntervention if applicable.

title: Estimands
text:
Feature keys, extra keys
The Estimand dataclass has these fields:
@dataclass
class Estimand:
    feature_keys: tuple[str, ...]      # columns alpha is indexed by
    m: Callable[..., Any]              # the linear functional
    extra_keys: tuple[str, ...] = ()   # per-row payload columns (e.g. shift_samples)
    name: str = "custom"
    factory_spec: dict | None = None   # set by built-in factories so the estimand
                                       # round-trips through save / load
The estimator reads feature_keys and extra_keys to assemble the row dicts passed to m.

title: Install
text: rieszreg holds the shared abstractions; pick at least one implementation package for an actual fitter.

title: Install
text:
Python
The four packages live in sibling GitHub repos: rieszreg (meta-package + this user guide), rieszboost, krrr, forestriesz. Clone them into a parent directory:
mkdir RieszReg && cd RieszReg
git clone https://github.com/rieszreg/rieszreg.git
git clone https://github.com/rieszreg/rieszboost.git
git clone https://github.com/rieszreg/krrr.git
git clone https://github.com/rieszreg/forestriesz.git
python3 -m venv .venv
.venv/bin/pip install -e rieszreg/python
.venv/bin/pip install -e rieszboost/python      # gradient boosting backend
.venv/bin/pip install -e krrr/python            # kernel ridge backend
.venv/bin/pip install -e forestriesz/python     # random-forest backend
rieszboost’s XGBoostBackend requires OpenMP. On macOS:
brew install libomp
Linux distributions (and GitHub Actions ubuntu-latest) ship libgomp1; no extra step is needed. krrr has no system prerequisites; an optional GPU backend uses falkon (pip install -e 'krrr/python[falkon]'). forestriesz depends on econml, which ships prebuilt wheels for macOS arm64+x86_64 and ubuntu — no compiler required.

Verify

PythonR



import rieszreg, rieszboost
from rieszreg import ATE, RieszEstimator
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
print("rieszreg", rieszreg.__version__ if hasattr(rieszreg, '__version__') else "OK")

rieszreg OK

print("rieszboost", "OK")

rieszboost OK





print("rieszboost (R wrapper) OK")

[1] "rieszboost (R wrapper) OK"

title: Install
text:
R wrappers (optional)
The R packages are R6 wrappers that call the Python implementations through reticulate. They do not require any separate algorithmic dependency.
First-time setup:

Install the R package dependencies:
install.packages(c("reticulate", "R6"))
Load the R wrappers. For this guide we dev-load them from the source tree:
pkgload::load_all("rieszboost/r/rieszboost")
pkgload::load_all("krrr/r/krrr")
pkgload::load_all("forestriesz/r/forestriesz")
Point reticulate at the Python venv:
use_python_rieszboost(".venv/bin/python")
Or set the environment variable before R starts:
export RETICULATE_PYTHON=/path/to/RieszReg/.venv/bin/python

The R API exposes every built-in estimand, every loss, and the full estimator interface for each backend. The symbolic tracer for custom m() is Python-only; see r-interface for how to call it from R.

title: Install
text:
Tests
.venv/bin/python -m pytest rieszboost/python/tests -v
.venv/bin/python -m pytest krrr/python/tests -v
.venv/bin/python -m pytest forestriesz/python/tests -v

Rscript -e '
  Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = file.path(getwd(), ".venv/bin/python"))
  pkgload::load_all("rieszreg/r/rieszreg")
  pkgload::load_all("rieszboost/r/rieszboost")
  testthat::test_dir("rieszboost/r/rieszboost/tests/testthat")
  pkgload::load_all("krrr/r/krrr")
  testthat::test_dir("krrr/r/krrr/tests/testthat")
  pkgload::load_all("forestriesz/r/forestriesz")
  testthat::test_dir("forestriesz/r/forestriesz/tests/testthat")
'

title: Losses
text: The Bregman-Riesz loss generalizes the squared Riesz loss via a strictly convex potential \(\phi\). rieszreg ships four built-in LossSpec classes; backends consume per-row gradient and Hessian sourced from the loss spec.

title: Losses
text:
Loss table










Loss
Potential \(\phi\)
Link \(\eta \to \alpha\)
Domain
Use for




SquaredLoss
\(t^2\)
identity
\(\mathbb{R}\)
Default.


KLLoss
\(t \log t\)
exp
\((0, \infty)\)
Density-ratio targets (TSM, IPSI). Guarantees \(\hat\alpha > 0\). Requires non-negative \(m\)-coefficients.


BernoulliLoss
\(t \log t + (1-t)\log(1-t)\)
sigmoid
\((0, 1)\)
\(\alpha_0\) known to lie in \((0, 1)\). Same coefficient-sign restriction as KLLoss.


BoundedSquaredLoss(lo, hi)
\(t^2\) + sigmoid-scaled link
\(\mathrm{lo} + (\mathrm{hi}-\mathrm{lo}) \cdot \sigma\)
\((\mathrm{lo}, \mathrm{hi})\)
Squared-loss fit with prior bounds (e.g. trimmed-propensity ratios).



SquaredLoss is the standard Lee-Schuler / Chernozhukov objective. Per-row loss in \(\alpha\)-space: \(a \cdot \alpha^2 + b \cdot \alpha\).

title: Losses
text:
KLLoss for density-ratio targets
KLLoss (\(\phi = t \log t\), exp link) targets density ratios. The exponential link keeps \(\hat\alpha > 0\).

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import KLLoss, TSM

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=KLLoss(max_eta=50),
    n_estimators=300,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=3,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"min α̂ = {alpha_hat.min():.4f}")

min α̂ = 0.1174

print(f"mean α̂ on treated rows = {alpha_hat[a == 1].mean():.3f}")

mean α̂ on treated rows = 1.845





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = KLLoss(max_eta = 50),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("min alpha_hat = %.4f\n", min(alpha_hat)))

min alpha_hat = 0.1173

cat(sprintf("mean alpha_hat on treated rows = %.3f\n",
            mean(alpha_hat[a == 1])))

mean alpha_hat on treated rows = 1.867





KLLoss requires all augmented b coefficients \(\le 0\), equivalent to all \(m\)-coefficients being non-negative. The check happens at fit time and raises with a clear error if violated.






WarningWhen KLLoss can be unstable



Pure-counterfactual rows (\(a=0\), \(b<0\)) have no minimum in \(\eta\) under KL — leaf weights at low-overlap points can extrapolate. Use SquaredLoss unless the positivity guarantee is needed.

title: Losses
text:
BoundedSquaredLoss for hard prior bounds
If you know \(\alpha_0 \in [\text{lo}, \text{hi}]\) (e.g. you’ve trimmed propensities to \([\varepsilon, 1-\varepsilon]\) so \(1/\pi \in [1, 1/\varepsilon]\)), BoundedSquaredLoss(lo, hi) keeps predictions in that box via a sigmoid-scaled link. The per-row loss in \(\alpha\)-space matches SquaredLoss; the link reparameterizes \(\eta\) so \(\hat\alpha\) saturates at the bounds rather than extrapolating past them.

PythonR



from rieszreg import BoundedSquaredLoss
booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=BoundedSquaredLoss(lo=0.0, hi=12.0),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=3,
).fit(df)
alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"alpha_hat in ({alpha_hat.min():.3f}, {alpha_hat.max():.3f})")

alpha_hat in (0.267, 11.751)





booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = BoundedSquaredLoss(lo = 0.0, hi = 12.0),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("alpha_hat in (%.3f, %.3f)\n",
            min(alpha_hat), max(alpha_hat)))

alpha_hat in (0.267, 11.812)











TipPick bounds tightly



Generous bounds make the sigmoid link saturate over most of \(\eta\), so boosting steps barely move predictions. Set lo and hi to your prior on \(\alpha_0\).


BernoulliLoss is the same construction with \(\text{lo}=0\), \(\text{hi}=1\) and the Bernoulli Bregman potential. Use it when \(\alpha_0\) is a probability.

title: Losses
R interface
text:
Each implementation package ships an R6 wrapper that calls the Python implementation through reticulate. Predictions are bitwise-identical to the Python results on the same input.
The R API is covered throughout the guide via the bilingual tabsets: see install for setup, quickstart for the standard fit pattern, and estimands for the full estimand inventory. This page is the focused R reference.

title: R interface
text:
Setup
After installing the Python packages (see install):
# Option A: env var, set before R starts
Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = "/path/to/RieszReg/.venv/bin/python")

# Option B: explicit, after R has started
use_python_rieszboost("/path/to/RieszReg/.venv/bin/python")
# or, for the kernel backend:
use_python_krrr("/path/to/RieszReg/.venv/bin/python")
# or, for the forest backend:
use_python_forestriesz("/path/to/RieszReg/.venv/bin/python")
use_python_<pkg>() accepts a path to a Python interpreter or a virtualenv directory and forwards to the appropriate reticulate function. It also imports the Python package so subsequent calls have no per-call import overhead.

API surface (forestriesz)







R
Python equivalent




ForestRieszRegressor$new(estimand, n_estimators, max_depth, min_samples_leaf, honest, inference, ...)
forestriesz.ForestRieszRegressor(...)

ForestRieszRegressor.load(path)



The R wrapper exposes the moment-style ForestRieszRegressor (single-basis fits — handles ATE/ATT/TSM via the auto-resolved sieve in Python). The augmentation-style AugForestRieszRegressor is Python-only in v1; call it from R via reticulate when you need a fully estimand-agnostic forest fit.
R6’s $method() syntax maps to Python’s .method(). Integer-typed kwargs (n_estimators, max_depth, random_state, early_stopping_rounds, n_landmarks, n_features, cg_max_iter, min_samples_leaf, subforest_size) take an L suffix in R: n_estimators = 200L. Other kwargs are auto-converted by reticulate.

title: R interface
text:
Parity
The same fit produces the same predictions in both languages. The R test suites verify this with tolerance = 1e-12.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 500
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
a = rng.binomial(1, 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(2 * x - 1))))
df_py = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster_py = RieszBooster(estimand=ATE(), n_estimators=50, learning_rate=0.05,
                          max_depth=3, random_state=42).fit(df_py)
alpha_py = booster_py.predict(df_py)
alpha_py[:5]

array([ 1.4076698,  1.4764098,  1.6470399, -1.1931754,  1.1374395],
      dtype=float32)





set.seed(0)
n  <- 500
x  <- runif(n)
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, 1 / (1 + exp(-(2 * x - 1))))
df_r <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster_r <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(), n_estimators = 50L, learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L, random_state = 42L
)
booster_r$fit(df_r)
alpha_r <- booster_r$predict(df_r)
head(alpha_r, 5)

[1]  1.212691 -1.263393  1.486148 -1.365574  1.212691





The two columns above use different RNGs (default_rng(0) versus set.seed(0)), so the df and the predictions differ between tabs. To verify parity end-to-end, fit on the same data in both languages and compare predictions row-by-row.

title: R interface
text:
Custom Python objects from R
The LinearForm tracer is Python-only. To use a custom estimand or LossSpec subclass from R, define it in Python via reticulate:
reticulate::py_run_string("
def m_my_thing(z, alpha):
    return 2.0 * (alpha(a=2, x=z['x']) - alpha(a=0, x=z['x']))

from rieszreg import Estimand
my_estimand = Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=m_my_thing, name='MyThing')
")

est <- reticulate::py$my_estimand
booster <- RieszBooster$new(estimand = est, n_estimators = 100L, max_depth = 3L)
booster$fit(df_r)
booster$predict(df_r)[1:5]
The same pattern works for custom LossSpec subclasses: subclass in Python and pass the instance via loss=.

title: R interface
text:
Reaching the Python object directly
booster$py is the underlying Python estimator. Use it for attributes the R6 wrapper doesn’t expose:
booster <- RieszBooster$new(estimand = ATE(), n_estimators = 50L)
booster$fit(df_r)

reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$best_iteration_)
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$best_score_)
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$base_score_)

title: Save and load
text:
A fitted Riesz estimator can be persisted two ways:
Both paths round-trip every built-in estimand and loss automatically.
The shared metadata format is defined in rieszreg.estimator.RieszEstimator.save. Backends register their Predictor loader at import time so the inherited load(...) classmethod can hydrate them by predictor_kind.

title: Save and load
text:
sklearn idiom: joblib.dump

PythonR



import joblib, tempfile, pathlib
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 800
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
).fit(df)
preds_before = booster.predict(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    p = pathlib.Path(td) / "booster.pkl"
    joblib.dump(booster, p)
    loaded = joblib.load(p)
    print("predictions match :", np.array_equal(preds_before, loaded.predict(df)))

    # Loaded booster still composes with sklearn:
    from sklearn.base import clone
    cloned = clone(loaded)
    print("clone OK          :", cloned.estimand.name)

['/var/folders/07/xf4cwwjd7nggpmy8y006ww6w0000gq/T/tmpjnofcrjn/booster.pkl']
predictions match : True
clone OK          : ATE




For R-side workflows, use the directory format below. To pickle from R via reticulate:
joblib <- reticulate::import("joblib")
joblib$dump(booster$py, "booster.pkl")
loaded_py <- joblib$load("booster.pkl")
# Wrap loaded_py back into an R6 RieszBooster if needed.

title: Save and load
text:
Portable directory format

PythonR



import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tempfile, pathlib
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 800
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02*x - x**2 + 4*np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
    early_stopping_rounds=20, validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)
preds_before = booster.predict(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    save_path = pathlib.Path(td) / "my_alpha"
    booster.save(save_path)
    print("Saved files:", sorted(p.name for p in save_path.iterdir()))

    loaded = RieszBooster.load(save_path)
    preds_after = loaded.predict(df)
    print(f"predictions match exactly: {np.array_equal(preds_before, preds_after)}")
    print(f"best_iteration preserved : {loaded.best_iteration_}")

title: Save and load
text:
Cross-language
Save in one language, load in the other. The backend payload is in its native format (xgboost UBJSON, joblib, kernel npz), and the metadata sidecar is plain JSON.
# In R: save the booster
booster$save("alpha_ate")
# In Python: load the same directory and continue working
loaded = RieszBooster.load("alpha_ate")
loaded.predict(new_df)
The R parity test exercises both directions with tolerance = 1e-12. The Python suite asserts that predictions are bitwise-identical pre- and post-save.

title: Save and load
text:
Custom estimands
Custom (non-built-in) Estimands can’t be reconstructed from JSON — the user’s m() callable doesn’t survive a save/load cycle. The estimator file itself is fine; you just have to re-pass the estimand on load:

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tempfile, pathlib
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import Estimand

# Set up data and a custom estimand (not a built-in)
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 200
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": rng.binomial(1, 0.5, n).astype(float),
                   "x": rng.uniform(0, 1, n)})

def m_custom(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) - alpha(a=0, x=z["x"])

custom = Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=m_custom, name="my_custom")
booster = RieszBooster(estimand=custom, n_estimators=20).fit(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    p = pathlib.Path(td) / "custom"
    booster.save(p)

    # Loading without the original estimand raises:
    try:
        RieszBooster.load(p)
    except ValueError as e:
        print("expected error:", str(e)[:80], "...")

    # Pass the original estimand to reconstruct:
    loaded = RieszBooster.load(p, estimand=custom)
    print("loaded with custom estimand OK")

expected error: Saved estimator at /var/folders/07/xf4cwwjd7nggpmy8y006ww6w0000gq/T/tmpk5r9505u/ ...
loaded with custom estimand OK

title: Save and load
text:
What’s in the file
Per-backend binary payload, plus metadata.json containing:

predictor_kind: "xgboost" (rieszboost), "sklearn" (rieszboost), "krrr" (krrr), "forestriesz" (forestriesz moment-style), or "aug-forestriesz" (forestriesz augmentation-style).
loss: {"type": "SquaredLoss" | "KLLoss" | …, "args": {…}} — re-instantiated with loss_from_spec(...) on load.
estimand_factory_spec: {"factory": "ATE" | …} for built-ins, null for custom.
feature_keys / extra_keys, base_score, best_iteration, best_score.
estimator_class and hyperparameters — informational; lets get_params(deep=False) on the loaded estimator mirror what you fit with.

The JSON is plain text and diffable; if the file format ever changes, rieszreg_format_version lets us migrate.
